Subject: Business Organization and Management 2nd Paper
Stem: Sobuj Bangla' is an agricultural machinery manufacturing enterprise. The organization manufactures 120 rice threshing machines every year. By the end of June 2024, it had produced 48 machines. Upon investigating the reasons behind not achieving the target, it was revealed that there was a shortage of laborers in the production department. Subsequently, the company recruited the necessary laborers and resumed production as per the rules.
Question_B: Why is control called the basis of planning? Explain.
Question_C: Using the information from the stem, determine the standard benchmark of production, the actual performance, and the deviation.
Question_D: Do you think that the enterprise in the stem has followed all the steps of control correctly? Analyze your opinion.
Ans_A: Break-Even Point (BEP) analysis is a financial control technique used to determine the specific level of production or sales volume at which an enterprise's total revenue exactly equals its total expenses, resulting in neither a profit nor a loss.
Ans_B:
Knowledge: Control is called the basis of planning because the final results, performance data, and corrective insights generated at the end of a control cycle serve as the factual starting point for designing subsequent organizational plans.


Realization: Management operates in a continuous loop where planning and control are deeply linked. Planning sets the future path, but it can be unrealistic if based on guesswork. Through control, managers discover why previous targets failed, identify structural bottlenecks, and evaluate the actual operational capacity of the workforce. These historical facts and realistic data points are fed directly into the next planning stage, ensuring future goals are accurate, attainable, and responsive to market realities.
Ans_C:
Knowledge (Para 1): Based on the data provided in the stem, the annual standard benchmark is 120 machines (pro-rated to 60 machines for 6 months), the actual performance is 48 machines, and the resulting performance deviation is a deficit of 12 machines.


Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To accurately evaluate the control parameters, we must analyze the data for the mid-year mark (end of June):

• Annual Production Standard = 120 rice threshing machines

• Pro-rated Mid-year Standard Target (6 months out of 12) = $120 \times \frac{6}{12} = 60 \text{ machines}$

• Actual Performance achieved by the end of June 2024 = 48 machines

• Performance Deviation = Actual Performance $-$ Mid-year Standard Target

• Deviation = $48 - 60 = -12 \text{ machines}$ (Deficit)


Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In management control, standard benchmarks represent the planned expectations, actual results represent real-world output, and deviation is the mathematical gap between them. The calculation proves that Sobuj Bangla experienced a negative deviation of 12 units at the mid-year milestone, meaning production lagged behind schedule by exactly 20% ($\frac{12}{60} \times 100$). This gap serves as the critical diagnostic indicator that flagged operational inefficiency in the factory.
Ans_D:
Knowledge (Para 1): Yes, I believe that the management of Sobuj Bangla has systematically and correctly followed all the core chronological steps of the control process to save their annual target.


Contextual Analysis (Para 2): The control loop is not a single action but a sequence of steps. The stem shows that Sobuj Bangla didn't just look at their low numbers and worry. They set a baseline (120 units), checked real progress in June (48 units), mathematically confirmed a shortfall, investigated the factory floor to find the root cause (labor shortage), and immediately fixed the issue by hiring new workers to resume normal production speed.


Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): The enterprise's perfect alignment with the standardized steps of control is verified through the following operational phases:

• Step 1: Setting Standards (মানদণ্ড নির্ধারণ): They established a clear, quantifiable target of manufacturing 120 rice threshing machines per year.

• Step 2: Measuring Actual Performance (প্রকৃত কার্যফল পরিমাপ): At the mid-year mark, management audited the inventory and recorded that exactly 48 machines had been built.

• Step 3: Comparing Performance with Standards (মানদণ্ডের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফলের তুলনা): Management compared the 48 actual units against the pro-rated mid-year expectation of 60 units, successfully identifying a negative deviation of 12 units.

• Step 4: Analyzing Deviations for Root Causes (বিচ্যুতির কারণ অনুসন্ধান ও বিশ্লেষণ): Instead of making unverified assumptions, they investigated the production department and isolated the exact problem—a critical shortage of factory workers.

• Step 5: Taking Corrective Actions (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ): They closed the loop by running a recruitment drive, hiring the missing manpower, and bringing the assembly line back to full capacity.


Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Sobuj Bangla stands as an ideal example of effective administrative control. Many organizations fail because they stop after discovering an error, leaving the problem unfixed. By turning their analytical data into rapid field correction—recruiting laborers to close the 12-unit gap—the management successfully completed the control loop, making their ultimate goal of producing 120 machines fully attainable before the year ends.

Subject: Business Organization and Management 2nd Paper
Stem: Ms. Mahmuda, the Managing Director of 'M/S Firoza Garments', fixed a target to manufacture 48,000 pieces of jackets at the beginning of the year. In her factory, manufacturing operations run 8 hours a day for 240 working days in a year. At the end of the year, the enterprise was able to manufacture 40,000 pieces of jackets. Ms. Mahmuda identified the reasons behind the lower production compared to the target and arranged for necessary training for the relevant employees of the production department along with installing modern machinery in the factory.
Question_B: "Planning is forward-looking and control is backward-looking" — Explain.
Question_C: According to the production target in the stem, how many jackets must be manufactured per hour?
Question_D: Evaluate the logical justification of the activities undertaken by Ms. Mahmuda to achieve the desired target in light of the steps of the control process.
Ans_A: Centralization is the administrative practice of retaining major decision-making power, authority, and control exclusively in the hands of top-level management, rather than distributing it to subordinate levels.
Ans_B:
Knowledge: Planning is forward-looking because it designs a blueprint for future actions, while control is backward-looking because it reviews past performance data against those pre-established blueprints to correct mistakes.


Realization: Management begins with planning, which involves looking ahead to set goals, strategies, and tasks for an upcoming period. Conversely, control is the final step where managers look back at what has already been executed. Control examines historical outputs, measures real-world performance gaps, and traces errors back to their roots. This backward review is vital because it reveals why past assumptions failed, generating factual insights that serve as the foundation for the next forward-looking plan.
Ans_C:
Knowledge (Para 1): Based on the production target and operational schedule of M/S Firoza Garments, the factory needs to manufacture 25 jackets per hour to meet its goals.


Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To find the hourly production standard, we must calculate the total annual operating hours and divide the target volume by that amount:

• Total planned production target = 48,000 jackets

• Total working days in a year = 240 days

• Daily operating hours = 8 hours

• Total annual production hours = $240 \text{ days} \times 8 \text{ hours/day} = 1,920 \text{ hours}$

• Required production per hour = $\frac{\text{Total Production Target}}{\text{Total Annual Hours}}$

• Hourly Output Standard = $\frac{48,000 \text{ jackets}}{1,920 \text{ hours}} = 25 \text{ jackets/hour}$


Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In administrative control, setting an hourly breakdown is an essential part of creating standard benchmarks. The calculation shows that the factory floor must maintain a steady speed of turning out exactly 25 jackets every single operating hour. This clear baseline allows shop supervisors to easily track daily output data and spot manufacturing delays early.
Ans_D:
Knowledge (Para 1): The activities undertaken by Ms. Mahmuda—installing modern machinery and arranging employee training—are highly justified because they represent the final and most decisive step of the control process: "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ).


Contextual Analysis (Para 2): At the end of the year, M/S Firoza Garments faced a negative performance deviation of 8,000 jackets ($40,000 - 48,000$). Instead of ignoring this gap, Ms. Mahmuda systematically investigated the issue, traced the deficit back to backdated equipment and technical skill gaps, and rolled out structural upgrades to fix the problem permanently.


Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Her corrective actions align perfectly with the steps of the control loop due to the following strategic reasons:

• Completing the Control Loop: Control is completely useless without its final step—taking corrective action. Merely measuring a shortfall of 8,000 units is a diagnostic warning; actual success comes from intervening to fix the underlying system flaws.

• Eliminating Root Technological Constraints: By installing modern machinery, Ms. Mahmuda removed physical capacity limits, allowing the factory floor to increase its hourly output well past the old standard of 25 jackets.

• Optimizing Human Capital Capacity: New machinery requires updated skills. Providing targeted technical training ensures that operators can handle the modern equipment efficiently, reducing errors and material waste.

• Establishing Factual Data for Future Planning: By upgrading both hardware and human skills, she creates a more capable baseline for her next planning cycle, ensuring future high-volume targets are fully realistic and attainable.


Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Ms. Mahmuda’s decisive response serves as an ideal example of effective management control. Many business owners fail because they stop after discovering a shortfall, leaving the actual problems unaddressed. By turning her analytical performance data into active factory upgrades, she successfully modernized her enterprise, ensuring that M/S Firoza Garments can hit its long-term targets and stay highly competitive in the market.

Subject: Business Organization and Management 2nd Paper
Stem: Sumaiya Shu Ltd. is a reputed shoe manufacturing company. They have a good reputation in the market for producing high-quality shoes. Due to the increasing demand for products in the market, the company is trying to increase production. For this reason, the company increased its target and set a benchmark of 500,000 units per year. However, after six months, the authority observed that only 300,000 units of shoes had been sold. Therefore, under these circumstances, the company evaluated its production activities, corrected the deviations, and took the necessary measures. As a result, the company became successful.
Question_B: Why is control considered the basis of future planning? Explain.
Question_C: Which initial step of control did Sumaiya Company follow? Explain.
Question_D: "The company became successful because the steps of the control process were followed correctly" — Do you agree with this statement? Give your opinion.
Ans_A: Budgetary control is a financial control technique where an organization's future plans are prepared in numerical or financial terms (a budget), and actual performance is continuously measured and compared against this budget to correct any financial deviations.
Ans_B:
Knowledge: Control is considered the basis of future planning because the performance data, identified errors, and corrective lessons generated during the control phase serve as the primary factual foundation for designing the next plan.


Realization: Planning is purely futuristic and requires realistic benchmarks to succeed. Through control, a manager analyzes real-world performance gaps and isolates why previous assumptions failed. Rather than relying on guesswork, these insights provide top management with hard data on operational capability, resource limits, and market bottlenecks. This allows them to create more accurate and practical goals in the next planning cycle, seamlessly linking the two functions in a management loop.
Ans_C:
Knowledge (Para 1): Sumaiya Company initially followed the "Measurement of Actual Performance" (প্রকৃত কার্যফল পরিমাপ) and "Comparison of Performance with Standards" (মানদণ্ডের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফলের তুলনা) steps of the control process.


Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem indicates that the company established a clear baseline standard: an annual production target of 500,000 units. To evaluate their progress, management assessed the actual business figures at the six-month mark (mid-year interval) and observed that the enterprise had achieved an output/sales volume of 300,000 units.


Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): The control process systematically begins with setting standards, followed by measuring real-time output and comparing the two data points to detect any gaps. By actively calculating the mid-year volume (300,000 units) and evaluating it against the pro-rated annual benchmark (which would be 250,000 units for six months), Sumaiya Company executed the core measurement and comparison steps of control. This calculation allowed them to see their exact position relative to their target.
Ans_D:
Knowledge (Para 1): Yes, I completely agree with the statement. Sumaiya Company's turnaround and ultimate success are direct results of systematically executing the chronological steps of the control process, ending with "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ).


Contextual Analysis (Para 2): When the authorities reviewed operations at the six-month mark, they did not just gather data and leave it on paper. The stem highlights that they evaluated their production activities, identified why things were tracking a certain way, corrected the flaws, and took necessary measures. This active operational intervention is what steered the company to success.


Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Following the control process correctly ensured the company's success due to the following reasons:

• Completing the Control Loop: Control is completely useless without its final step—taking corrective action. Measuring performance or finding errors is simply a diagnostic warning; real success comes from intervening to fix the underlying system flaws.

• Early Deviation Detection: By checking progress at the six-month mark rather than waiting until the end of the year, management caught operational mismatches early. This left them with an excellent six-month window to deploy administrative fixes.

• Eliminating Technical and Structural Flaws: Evaluating production allowed them to find the root causes of supply issues. Fixing these errors meant the manufacturing floor could run at maximum efficiency without wasting resources.

• Capitalizing on Market Demand: Since the market was already experiencing a rise in demand, taking rapid corrective measures allowed the firm to supply the market on time, translating their administrative efficiency directly into higher sales and success.


Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, the case of Sumaiya Shu Ltd. stands as an ideal example of effective management control. Many businesses fail because they stop after discovering a deviation, leaving the actual problems unaddressed. By turning their analytical data into active floor upgrades and corrective actions, Sumaiya Company successfully completed the control loop, realigned their manufacturing speed with market demand, and turned an operational checkpoint into a massive institutional success.

Subject: Business Organization and Management 2nd Paper
Stem: Mr. Shohid manufactures 9,000 pieces of electric fans per month in his factory with the help of five machines. The production capacity of each machine in the factory is identical. He runs manufacturing operations 30 days a month. The daily operating capacity of each machine is 10 hours. Mr. Shohid observes and keeps accounts of the production activities every day and gives necessary instructions to the workers.
Question_A: What is the last function of the management process?
Question_B: "Control is a continuous process" — Explain.
Question_C: Based on the timeframe in the stem, what is the standard benchmark of production for each machine per hour?
Question_D: "Control is dependent on the operational plan" — Evaluate the justification of this statement in light of the stem.
Ans_A: Control is the last or final function of the management process.
Ans_B:
Knowledge: Control is called a continuous or ongoing process because it is an uninterrupted, repetitive managerial activity that must operate constantly throughout the entire life cycle of an organization.


Realization: Management cannot implement control just once and abandon it. As long as organizational operations exist, actual performance data must be continuously measured, compared against planned standards, and corrected. As soon as one production cycle concludes, a new one begins with new targets, and the monitoring loop must repeat itself. If this evaluation loop pauses even briefly, operational flaws will go unnoticed, leading to deviations and failure to achieve organizational goals.
Ans_C:
Knowledge (Para 1): Based on the production target and operational schedule in the stem, the standard benchmark of production for each machine is 6 pieces of electric fans per hour.


Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To find the hourly production standard per machine, we must calculate the total monthly operating hours for a single machine and divide its share of the target volume by that amount:

• Total monthly target for the factory = 9,000 fans

• Total number of machines = 5

• Monthly production target per machine = $\frac{9,000 \text{ fans}}{5 \text{ machines}} = 1,800 \text{ fans/machine}$

• Total working days in a month = 30 days

• Daily operating hours per machine = 10 hours

• Total monthly operating hours per machine = $30 \text{ days} \times 10 \text{ hours/day} = 300 \text{ hours}$

• Hourly production standard per machine = $\frac{1,800 \text{ fans}}{300 \text{ hours}} = 6 \text{ fans/hour}$


Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In management control, establishing an hourly breakdown per unit of machinery is an essential part of creating standard benchmarks. The calculation proves that for Mr. Shohid's factory to hit its macro target, each machine must maintain a steady, unyielding speed of turning out exactly 6 fans every single operating hour. This provides a clear, scannable baseline for shop-floor evaluation.
Ans_D:
Knowledge (Para 1): The statement "Control is dependent on the operational plan" is entirely justified and accurate. Planning sets the baseline targets and rules, making it the absolute prerequisite and foundation upon which the control process operates.


Contextual Analysis (Para 2): In the stem, Mr. Shohid is able to physically "observe and keep accounts of the production activities every day" because he has a structured monthly operational plan. This plan establishes that 5 identical machines must run for 10 hours a day across 30 days to output a precise target of 9,000 fans. Without these pre-calculated numerical blueprints, his daily supervisory rounds would have no baseline metrics to cross-verify against.


Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): The close relationship and dependence of control on planning can be analyzed through the following points:

• Plans as the Evaluation Yardstick: Control fundamentally means checking for mistakes and gaps. If Mr. Shohid had not planned the 9,000-unit target, he would have no way of knowing whether his daily factory output was too slow, too fast, or right on track.

• Guiding Supervisory Instructions: The stem notes that Mr. Shohid gives "necessary instructions" to his staff. These real-time corrections are guided directly by the original plan; he instructs workers to increase speed or adjust machine setups specifically to stay aligned with the monthly schedule.

• Preventing Arbitrary Management: Without an operational plan, control becomes blind guesswork. A manager cannot fairly judge worker efficiency or machine breakdowns unless there is an established standard, such as the 6 fans per hour per machine baseline, to hold operations accountable.


Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, planning and control are two sides of the same coin, where control is completely dependent on the plan. Planning acts as the initial guide post, and control ensures that the organization stays on the path that was mapped out. By utilizing his clear operational schedule to run daily field inspections, Mr. Shohid perfectly demonstrates how a solid plan enables effective administrative control, ensuring the factory achieves its commercial objectives on time.

Subject: Business Organization and Management 2nd Paper
Stem: Mr. Zakaria is the Sales Manager of Ohona Pharmaceuticals. At the beginning of the year, he sets sales targets for all regional managers. At the end of September, he visits the Pabna region and observes that only 65% of the target has been achieved. Mr. Zakaria identifies the reasons behind the lower target achievement of the Regional Manager of that region, Mr. Alim, evaluates it, and directs him to take subsequent measures to achieve the desired goal.
Question_A: Which step of the management process is control?
Question_B: Why is control called a continuous process? Explain.
Question_C: Which step of the control process did Mr. Zakaria follow at the beginning of the year? Explain.
Question_D: Give your opinion through the control process on what measures should be taken to make Mr. Alim's desired goal achievable.
Ans_A: Control is the last or final step of the management process.
Ans_B:
Knowledge: Control is called a continuous or ongoing process because it is an uninterrupted, repetitive managerial activity that must operate constantly throughout the entire lifecycle of an organization.


Realization: Management cannot implement control just once and abandon it. As long as organizational operations exist, actual performance data must be continuously measured, compared against planned standards, and corrected. As soon as one sales or production cycle concludes, a new one begins with fresh targets, and the monitoring loop must repeat itself. If this evaluation loop pauses even briefly, operational flaws will go unnoticed, leading to performance deviations and failure to achieve organizational goals.
Ans_C:
Knowledge (Para 1): At the beginning of the year, Mr. Zakaria followed the first step of the control process, which is "Setting Standard Benchmarks" (আদর্শমান নির্ধারণ).


Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem states that at the very start of the commercial cycle, Mr. Zakaria fixed specific, quantifiable "sales targets" for all his regional managers. This pre-determined volume serves as the corporate blueprint that dictates what each regional territory is expected to achieve.


Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Control cannot exist in a vacuum; it requires a baseline yardstick to measure success or failure later on. In management, these baseline metrics are established during the standard-setting phase, which borrows heavily from the initial planning blueprints. By setting clear numerical sales targets for his field managers at the beginning of the year, Mr. Zakaria established the necessary control standards that allowed him to evaluate the Pabna region's progress down the line.
Ans_D:
Knowledge (Para 1): To ensure Mr. Alim achieves his target, the management must rigorously implement the final step of the control process, "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ), focusing on territory support, field counseling, and supply chain optimizations.


Contextual Analysis (Para 2): At the end of September (9 months into the year), Mr. Alim's territory has only hit 65% of its sales target, revealing a negative performance deviation of 35%. Since the pro-rated expectation for 9 months is around 75%, he is facing a clear deficit. However, because Mr. Zakaria caught this gap with 3 months left in the year, there is an excellent window for recovery if active field interventions are deployed immediately.


Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Based on the control framework, Mr. Alim can execute the following specific corrective measures to salvage his annual target:

• Investigate Territorial Bottlenecks: Mr. Alim must sit down with his medical representatives to isolate the root cause of the slow sales. Is it due to aggressive competitor pricing, stock-outs of key medicines in Pabna pharmacies, or low doctor prescription rates?

• Deploy Aggressive Medical Detailing & Field Counseling: If the shortfall stems from low prescription volumes, Mr. Alim must personally join his reps on field visits to doctors and hospitals to improve their product pitching and build stronger professional relationships.

• Optimize Distribution & Product Availability: Management must ensure that distribution depots in the Pabna region are fully stocked. If life-saving drugs are unavailable when retail pharmacies order them, customers will easily switch to competing pharmaceutical brands.

• Offer Targeted Trade Discounts or Incentives: To push volumes in the final quarter, temporary incentive programs or retail bonuses can be offered to pharmacies to encourage them to clear stock and favor Ohona Pharmaceuticals' products.


Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, catching a 35% performance gap is merely a diagnostic warning; actual corporate success comes from the administrative power to change that trajectory. Merely ordering a manager to do better is useless unless it is backed by practical field correction. By turning Mr. Zakaria's analytical data into immediate corrective actions—such as running localized doctor campaigns and fixing supply delays—Mr. Alim can bridge the sales deficit and successfully hit his 100% annual target before the year ends.

Subject: Business Organization and Management 2nd Paper
Stem: Johnson Fashion World Limited is an export-oriented manufacturing enterprise. On January 15, the company accepted an order to supply 500,000 pieces of pants within 4 months. However, by March 20, the enterprise was only able to manufacture 200,000 pieces of pants. Under these circumstances, to ensure the timely delivery of the order, the production manager decided to take necessary measures after identifying the reasons behind not achieving the production target.
Question_B: Why is it necessary to set standard benchmarks in the control process? Explain.
Question_C: What kind of performance deviation was found in Johnson Fashion World Limited? Explain.
Question_D: "Following the proper steps of the control process made it possible to deliver the ordered goods on time" — Evaluate this statement in light of the stem.
Ans_A: Control is the systematic managerial function of measuring actual performance against pre-determined standards, identifying errors or deviations, and taking corrective actions to ensure organizational goals are met.
Ans_B:
Knowledge: It is necessary to set a standard benchmark because it serves as the essential baseline yardstick or criteria against which actual performance is measured and evaluated.


Realization: Control fundamentally involves checking whether organizational activities are progressing according to plan. Without a pre-determined standard (such as a budget limit, production target, or time deadline), managers have no data to compare actual results against. They cannot determine whether a project is succeeding or failing because there is no target baseline. Therefore, establishing standard benchmarks is an absolute prerequisite for initiating any control activity.
Ans_C:
Knowledge (Para 1): Johnson Fashion World Limited experienced a negative performance deviation (ঋণাত্মক বিচ্যুতি) amounting to a deficit of 62,500 pieces of pants at the checkpoint.


Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To calculate the exact deviation, we must figure out the pro-rated target for the elapsed time and compare it with the actual output:

• Total target = 500,000 pants in 4 months (or 120 days)

• Time elapsed from Jan 15 to Mar 20 = 16 days (Jan) + 28 days (Feb) + 20 days (Mar) = 64 days

• Standard production target per day = $\frac{500,000 \text{ pants}}{120 \text{ days}} = 4,166.67 \text{ pants/day}$

• Expected standard target for 64 days = $64 \text{ days} \times 4,166.67 \text{ pants/day} = 266,666.67 \text{ pants}$ (rounded to 262,500 pants if using a standard monthly split of 2.1 months out of 4 months: $\frac{500,000 \times 2.1}{4}$)

• Let's use standard monthly prorating for simplicity: Total timeline = 4 months. Time passed = 15 Jan to 20 March = 2.1 months. Prorated standard target = 262,500 pants.

• Actual performance achieved = 200,000 pants.

• Performance Deviation = Actual Performance $-$ Standard Target

• Deviation = $200,000 - 262,500 = -62,500 \text{ pieces}$ (Deficit).


Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In management, a negative deviation indicates that real-world execution is lagging behind the planned schedule. Johnson Fashion World Limited fell short of its milestone by exactly 62,500 pants. This negative gap acts as an administrative warning sign, showing that the current assembly line speed is too slow to meet the final export deadline.
Ans_D:
Knowledge (Para 1): The statement is entirely true and justified. By systematically following the chronological steps of control—culminating in "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ)—the production manager can successfully close the deficit and ensure timely shipment.


Contextual Analysis (Para 2): Finding themselves 62,500 pants behind schedule on March 20, the company still has nearly 2 months remaining before the mid-May deadline to manufacture the remaining 300,000 pieces ($500,000 - 200,000$). Instead of panicking, the production manager chose to follow proper control protocols by isolating the root causes of the delay and preparing targeted operational fixes.


Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Adhering to the systematic steps of control guarantees shipment success due to the following reasons:

• Moving from Analysis to Action: Discovering a negative deviation of 62,500 units is merely a diagnostic phase. The control process only adds value when management moves to the final step—deploying field corrections to fix the factory floor bottlenecks.

• Root Cause Eradication: By investigating why production lagged (e.g., mechanical faults, fabric supply delays, or worker absenteeism), the manager can fix the actual problems rather than applying superficial solutions.

• Ramping up Operational Capacity: To make 300,000 pants in the remaining 2 months, the manager can introduce double shifts, authorize overtime pay, or bring in temporary tailors to increase daily output speeds.

• Preserving International Trust: For an export-oriented garment firm, missing a deadline results in cancelled orders and financial penalties. Corrective control realigns sluggish floor outputs with international supply commitments.


Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, the production manager's decision to follow the structured steps of control is highly logical and practical. Finding a shortfall is simply a warning sign; actual corporate success comes from the administrative power to change that bad trajectory. By turning analytical control data into rapid interventions—such as increasing floor supervision and running extra shifts—the management can successfully speed up production, eliminate the 62,500-unit deficit, and deliver the shipment on time.

Subject: Business Organization and Management 2nd Paper
Stem: Mr. Masum has established a dairy farm. After completing all necessary arrangements, he started operations with a target production level of 900 liters of milk per month. However, at the end of the month, he observed that only 700 liters of milk had been produced. Later, through close and intensive supervision, he discovered that the workers were cunningly misappropriating the produced milk. Consequently, he terminated two workers and hired new staff.
Question_A: What is the basis of control?
Question_B: What is meant by standard benchmark (or standard)? Explain.
Question_C: Which step of the control process did Mr. Masum adopt to confirm the shortfall in production? Explain.
Question_D: Analyze the managerial function that Mr. Masum has executed appropriately in light of the stem.
Ans_A: Planning (পরিকল্পনা) is the basis of control.
Ans_B:
Knowledge: A standard benchmark (or standard) refers to the pre-determined model, target, or yardstick established during the planning phase, against which the actual performance of an organization is measured and evaluated.


Realization: Standards serve as the corporate baseline for judging operational success or failure. These metrics can be qualitative (such as product quality or customer satisfaction) or quantitative (such as a target of producing 1,000 units, or a budget limit). Without a clear, predetermined standard, it is impossible to evaluate real-world performance because there is no target baseline to check against. Therefore, setting standard benchmarks acts as the fundamental starting point of the control function.
Ans_C:
Knowledge (Para 1): Mr. Masum adopted the second step of the control process, which is "Measuring Actual Performance" (প্রকৃত কার্যফল পরিমাপ), and compared it with his target to confirm the production shortfall.


Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem indicates that Mr. Masum set an initial monthly production target of 900 liters of milk. At the end of the month, he evaluated his actual operational results and recorded that the farm had generated exactly 700 liters of milk. By putting these two real-world figures side by side, he pinpointed a clear deficit.


Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Performance measurement is the diagnostic phase of control where managers collect real-time field data and contrast it against the original planning targets. It is through this step that deviations become visible. By measuring the real yield (700 liters) against his benchmark (900 liters), Mr. Masum was able to mathematically prove a negative performance deviation of 200 liters, thereby confirming that the dairy farm was lagging behind its planned capacity.
Ans_D:
Knowledge (Para 1): Mr. Masum has flawlessly executed "Control" (নিয়ন্ত্রণ)—the final and corrective function of management—by identifying operational theft, isolating the root cause, and deploying strict administrative discipline.


Contextual Analysis (Para 2): After recording a 200-liter shortfall, Mr. Masum did not simply ignore the data or guess the reason. He conducted intensive, close floor-supervision to uncover the root cause, discovering that internal workers were stealthily stealing the milk. To address this severe ethical breakdown, he immediately completed the control loop by firing two corrupt workers and bringing in fresh personnel.


Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Mr. Masum’s actions perfectly illustrate why the control function is vital for an enterprise's survival:

• Moving from Analysis to Correction: Discovering a deficit is merely a diagnostic phase. The control process only adds value when management moves to the final step—deploying active interventions to fix the leakages.

• Protecting Corporate Assets: If Mr. Masum had only monitored the numbers without taking disciplinary actions, the workplace theft would have continued, leading to compounding financial losses and the eventual collapse of the dairy farm.

• Establishing Accountability: Firing the dishonest workers sends a clear message to the remaining staff. It restores workplace discipline, minimizes future inventory shrinkage, and ensures the farm can realistically hit its 900-liter baseline.


Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Mr. Masum's execution of management principles is highly logical and complete. Finding a shortfall is simply a warning sign; actual corporate success comes from the administrative power to change that trajectory. By turning raw monitoring data into direct corrective action—firing the bad actors and restructuring his workforce—he successfully protected his business investment and paved the way to achieve his desired goals.

Subject: Business Organization and Management 2nd Paper
Stem: Mrs. Fatiha is the production manager of an organization. She set a weekly production target of 100,000 units of products for her unit. At the end of the week, it was observed that the actual production of that unit was only 80,000 units. Under these circumstances, she is quite worried and is now considering revising the plan.
Question_B: Why is control called the basis of the next plan? Explain.
Question_C: At which step of the control process did Mrs. Fatiha notice the production shortfall? Explain.
Question_D: Provide the necessary advice to Mrs. Fatiha regarding the revision of the plan.
Ans_A: Control is the systematic managerial function of measuring actual performance against pre-determined standards, identifying errors or deviations, and taking corrective actions to ensure organizational goals are met.
Ans_B:
Knowledge: Control is called the basis of the next plan because the data, performance feedback, and corrective lessons gathered during the control stage provide the realistic foundation upon which future plans are designed.


Realization: Planning is the initial step of management, while control is the final step. However, management is a continuous cycle. When control reveals performance deviations, it investigates the root causes—such as unrealistic targets, resource shortages, or worker inefficiency. This vital diagnostic information ensures that the next planning cycle is realistic and grounded in actual field capability, rather than guesswork. Thus, control acts as a bridge, transforming past operational feedback into the starting point for future organizational strategies.
Ans_C:
Knowledge (Para 1): Mrs. Fatiha noticed the production shortfall during the third step of the control process, which is "Comparing Actual Performance with Standards" (আদর্শ মানের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফল তুলনাকরণ).


Contextual Connection (Para 2): According to the stem, Mrs. Fatiha had set a weekly standard target of 100,000 units. At the end of the cycle, she measured the real-world output and found it to be 80,000 units. By placing the actual performance side-by-side with her original plan, she mathematically exposed a negative deviation of 20,000 units ($80,000 - 100,000$).


Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Comparing performance is the specific diagnostic phase where managers contrast raw field data against pre-determined benchmarks to isolate gaps. Mrs. Fatiha could not have known there was a shortfall simply by looking at the 80,000 units alone; it was the active step of comparison against her 100,000-unit baseline that highlighted the 20,000-unit deficit and triggered her concern.
Ans_D:
Knowledge (Para 1): To successfully revise her plan, Mrs. Fatiha must transition from the diagnostic phase to "Taking Corrective Action" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ) by thoroughly auditing her production bottlenecks before adjusting her targets or resources.


Contextual Analysis (Para 2): Facing a substantial 20% shortfall (20,000 units), Mrs. Fatiha is rightfully worried. However, simply lowering the target or panicking will not solve the underlying factory floor issues. She must use systematic control principles to investigate why the gap occurred and implement logical administrative fixes.


Justification & Strategic Advice (Para 3): To effectively restructure her planning framework, Mrs. Fatiha should adopt the following measures:

• Investigate the Root Causes: Before changing any numbers, she must determine if the deficit was caused by internal inefficiencies (such as machinery breakdowns, worker absenteeism, or raw material delays) or an inherently unrealistic initial target.

• Evaluate and Optimize Resources: If the target of 100,000 units is necessary to meet market demand, she should advise the company to scale up operational capacity—such as introducing overtime pay, repairing old machinery, or adding an extra production shift.

• Formulate a Realistic, Flexible Plan: If her audit reveals that the factory floor's maximum physical capacity is lower than expected, she must revise her next weekly plan to a realistic baseline (e.g., 85,000 units) to avoid overpromising to clients.

• Establish Milestone-Based Monitoring: Instead of checking results only at the end of the week, she should implement daily production check-ins to catch and fix minor delays before they compound into a major weekend shortfall.


Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Mrs. Fatiha's inclination to revise her plan is highly logical, but it must be driven by data rather than anxiety. Identifying a shortfall is merely an administrative warning sign; actual corporate success comes from the power to change that trajectory. By turning her analytical control data into targeted interventions—either by boosting floor resources or adjusting future benchmarks to match real factory capacity—she will ensure the organization maintains steady, predictable, and highly efficient manufacturing outputs.

Subject: Business Organization and Management 2nd Paper
Stem: Mr. Shimu is the owner of a garments factory. He fixed a target to manufacture 15,000 pieces of clothing per month for his enterprise. Despite utilizing the full production capacity, the production in January and February was 13,000 and 13,500 pieces respectively. Upon investigation, a shortage of skilled laborers and raw materials was found to be the underlying reason. Following the supply of necessary skilled workers and raw materials, the enterprise succeeded in achieving its desired target starting from March.
Question_A: What is a terminal (or periodic) task?
Question_B: "Control is always base-dependent" — Explain.
Question_C: Through which step of control were the production figures of the enterprise in the stem obtained? Explain.
Question_D: "The target was achieved precisely due to the adoption of an effective control system" — Evaluate the justification of this statement.
Ans_A: A terminal (or periodic) task is an administrative activity or evaluation that is performed systematically at the very end of a specific, pre-determined operating period (such as a week, month, or fiscal year).
Ans_B:
Knowledge: Control is always base-dependent because it cannot function in a vacuum; it requires a pre-established plan or standard benchmark to act as its absolute foundation and yardstick.


Realization: The entire purpose of control is to evaluate whether business operations are on the right track. To make this judgment, a manager must have an existing baseline—such as a budget limit, a timeline, or a specific production target. Without this foundational plan, there are no criteria to measure actual performance against, making it impossible to identify deviations or errors. Therefore, planning acts as the necessary base that gives the control process its meaning and direction.
Ans_C:
Knowledge (Para 1): The performance figures of Mr. Shimu's factory were obtained through the second and third steps of the control process, which are "Measuring Actual Performance" (প্রকৃত কার্যফল পরিমাপ) and "Comparing Performance with Standards" (মানদণ্ডের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফলের তুলনা).


Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem indicates that Mr. Shimu set a clear baseline standard of 15,000 pieces of clothing per month. At the end of January and February, his management recorded the real-world factory outputs—13,000 and 13,500 pieces respectively. By actively matching these recorded figures against his original monthly target, he mathematically confirmed shortfalls of 2,000 and 1,500 pieces.


Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Performance measurement and comparison are the diagnostic phases of control where managers collect raw field data and place it side-by-side with planned benchmarks. A manager cannot naturally spot a deficit without this active pairing. By executing these steps, Mr. Shimu exposed the negative deviations, which served as the essential administrative warning signs needed before any structural changes could be made on the factory floor.
Ans_D:
Knowledge (Para 1): The statement is completely true and justified. The successful turnaround of the factory from March onwards is a direct result of executing the final step of the control loop: "Taking Timely and Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ).


Contextual Analysis (Para 2): When faced with production deficits in January and February despite using full capacity, Mr. Shimu did not simply ignore the data or rely on guesswork. He investigated the floor, traced the root causes to a shortage of skilled labor and raw materials, and immediately corrected the system by supplying the missing resources. This active intervention realigned his factory floor with his targets.


Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Implementing this effective control system guaranteed the enterprise's success due to the following strategic reasons:

• Completing the Control Loop: Control is completely useless without its final step—taking corrective action. Measuring performance or finding errors is simply a diagnostic phase; real success comes from intervening to fix the underlying system flaws.

• Eradicating Root Operational Bottlenecks: By directly addressing the shortages of skilled manpower and fabric, Mr. Shimu removed the actual physical constraints that were keeping his assembly lines from hitting their true operating potential.

• Optimizing Resource Efficiency: Supplying qualified personnel reduced material waste and manufacturing delays, allowing the factory to easily convert its raw material inputs into finished, market-ready goods.

• Preventing Compounding Financial Losses: If he had lacked a control system, the 2,000-unit deficit would have continued indefinitely, leading to missed client deadlines, cancelled export orders, and severe financial losses.


Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Mr. Shimu's business turnaround serves as an ideal example of effective management control. Identifying a production shortfall is merely a warning sign; actual corporate success comes from the administrative power to change that trajectory. By turning raw monitoring numbers into rapid field corrections—hiring skilled labor and stabilizing the raw material supply chain—management successfully closed the performance gap, making their ultimate commercial goal fully achievable from March onward.

Subject: Business Organization and Management 2nd Paper
Stem:
The diagram in the image "image_94f148.png" illustrates a stair-step flowchart detailing the chronological stages of the management control process:

1. Setting Standard Benchmarks (আদর্শমান নির্ধারণ)

2. Measuring Actual Performance (কার্যফল পরিমাপ)

3. 'C' (গ)

4. Determining and Evaluating Reasons for Deviation (বিচ্যুতির কারণ নির্ণয় ও মূল্যায়ন)

5. Taking Corrective Actions (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ)
Question_B: "Control activity is base-dependent" — Explain.
Question_C: Explain what is represented by the placeholder 'C' (গ) in the stem's diagram.
Question_D: Analyze the effectiveness and productivity of following the steps shown in the stem's flowchart.
Ans_A: A budget is the quantitative or numerical expression of a future operational plan formulated for a specific pre-determined period.
Ans_B:

Realization: The entire purpose of control is to check whether business operations are moving in the right direction. To make this judgment, a manager must have a baseline—such as a budget ceiling, a project timeline, or a production volume target. Without this foundational plan, there are no criteria to measure actual performance against, making it impossible to identify errors. Therefore, planning serves as the indispensable base that gives the control process its meaning and functional direction.
Ans_C:
Knowledge (Para 1): In the provided diagram from the image "image_94f148.png", the placeholder 'C' (গ) represents the third chronological step of the control process: "Comparing Actual Performance with Standards and Identifying Deviations" (আদর্শমানের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফলের তুলনা ও বিচ্যুতি নির্ধারণ).


Theoretical Connection (Para 2): As shown in the step-by-step ladder layout of the flowchart, this function sits directly above measuring performance and directly below analyzing errors. Once a manager has established the target benchmarks (Step 1) and gathered the real-world field metrics (Step 2), they must place these two sets of data side-by-side to cross-examine and mathematically compute the variation between them.


Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): This step acts as the administrative bridge of the entire control loop. A manager cannot naturally spot a deficit or a surplus by looking at field data alone; it requires active matching against the baseline standard. By identifying whether the resulting deviation is positive, neutral, or negative, this step provides the raw analytical material required for Step 4 (investigating root causes) and Step 5 (taking corrective action).
Ans_D:

Analytical Evaluation (Para 2): The control process is designed as an interlocking sequence where each step depends completely on the one before it. Skipping any phase breaks the chain. For example, trying to take corrective actions (Step 5) without knowing the root cause (Step 4) or without calculating the exact deviation (Step 3) will lead to arbitrary management decisions that waste company resources.


Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): The productivity and institutional benefits of adhering strictly to this structured flowchart include:

• Early Warning and Risk Mitigation: By systematically measuring performance (Step 2) and comparing it with benchmarks (Step 3), managers can spot operational delays or supply deficits early, giving them a window to fix problems before they compound into financial failure.

• Data-Driven Decision Making: Investigating the root causes of performance gaps (Step 4) ensures that managers target the actual bottleneck—such as repairing faulty machines or training slow workers—rather than applying superficial or useless remedies.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, the stair-step framework illustrated in the image "image_94f148.png" is the ultimate diagnostic and therapeutic tool for corporate health. Identifying an operational problem is merely a warning sign; actual institutional survival depends on the power to systematically trace and fix that error.
